steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: This is a Form, Structure, and Sense question testing the it's / its / they're / their distinction. The blank begins a clause that must have a subject and a linking verb to complete the sentence grammatically. The subject referred to is the Spanish constitution (singular).

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘its’: Possessive pronoun. It cannot serve as the subject of a clause; it would need a noun after it (e.g., "its rank is"). Without one, the clause has no verb. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘it's’: Contraction of "it is." This provides both the subject ("it") and the linking verb ("is"), completing the clause as "it is 105th in a global ranking of the shortest constitutions." Singular, matching the Spanish constitution. ✓
  • Choice C — ‘they're’: Contraction of "they are." Plural — does not agree with the singular subject (the Spanish constitution). ✗
  • Choice D — ‘their’: Possessive pronoun. Like "its," it cannot serve as the subject of the clause and has no noun to possess here. Also plural, mismatching the singular antecedent. ✗
That confirms B is correct.
